other_observers: Joe Roller and a couple other birders I didn't know; Christian Nunes found the bird.
description: The bird was clearly a Sterna tern -- about half the size of the Ring-billed Gulls on the reservoir, with wingbeats about twice as fast, mostly white plumage, pointed wings and a deeply forked tail. It had a medium-length, thin, pointed, mostly dark bill and a neat black cap that came from the bill through the eyes and around the back of the head, including much of the crown and the nape. Its wings were almost entirely whitish, with only hints of darker markings, like a very thin black tip to the outer primaries visible from below only at close range, and a dark (not quite black) carpal bar on the upperwing with a slight brownish wash around it, also only really visible at close range. The palest part of the upperwing was the central trailing portion, which was silvery-white, a triangular region on the wing roughly in the same place as the white on a Sabine's Gull wing.Behaviors: The bird flew all around the reservoir, usually within 20 feet of the surface, occasionally wheeling and diving down to the water's surface to pick something off the water. Its flight was fast and its wingbeats rapid and choppy, especially compared to the comparatively lumbering flight of the Ring-billed Gulls around it.Call: Bird was silent.Plumage: probably juvenile
